pages.raw_text:
Editor, The Press-The fame OF
Spokane has spread throughout the
United States, not only for progress
siveness and enterprise but for the
beauty of its site. Visitors vie with
each other in praising the natural
endowment Of the City Beautiful"
Noone doubts that Spokane is de.
stined to become a great city. Is
II not high time that we profit by
the experience of the older cities
and plan a park system that will
be adequate to the needs OF the fu'
ture and that will preserve our
natural scenery?


must confess that ] am astonish'
ed that no protest las been raised
against the proposal of one of our
traction corporations to desecrate
and obliterate what God manifestly
intended for a public park. Is it
not enough that the falls has been
partly sacrificed for industrial pur-
poses? The chief glory of Spokane
is the falls and the river gorge be-
low. Must we suffer it to be for
ever hidden and profaned by the
hand of greed? II there is really
any public spirit in this city we will
condemn the Spokane falls and
gorge for park purposes before it is
too late and maintain them forever
for the benefit and enjoyment OF
all the people. 1t seems incredible
that we should supinely tolerate for
an instant this vandalism; this
dastardly plan to ravish our fail
city of it's greatest scenic beauty
Let us hear from those who are
boosting for Greater Spokane
Wake up! J. E. Arnett.
